question: The console gates model/council changes for the harness. v1 network exposure is tailnet-only (same as the dashboard), and the ask was an 'oauth console' but the account has no domain yet, so real OIDC cannot terminate anywhere public. Options: (a) bearer token from a 0600 file on the box, checked by a single verify_request() seam, with OAuth documented as a later swap of that one function; (b) scaffold an OAuth device-flow now against a to-be-registered provider with the token path as fallback (more code, dead until a provider app exists); (c) trust tailnet identity headers only (no app-level credential at all). Which posture for v1, and why?
synthesis: Adopt Option (a): a bearer token stored in a 0600 file on the box, verified through a single verify_request() seam, with OAuth documented as a later swap of that one function. This is the correct v1 posture because tailnet-only exposure already minimizes the attack surface, and a file-based bearer token adds lightweight, immediately testable defense-in-depth without depending on a yet-to-exist OAuth provider or a public domain. Option (b) introduces dead, untested code paths that will rot before any provider app exists. Option (c) creates an invisible coupling to tailnet proxy behavior with no app-level fallback if ACLs or proxy configuration drift. The verify_request() seam keeps the future OAuth migration isolated to one well-defined function, minimizing rework. Enforce strict 0600 ownership, never log the token, and keep the seam minimal.
contradictions: Whether the single-seam abstraction genuinely enables a clean future OAuth swap or whether 'documented for later' becomes permanent tech debt: Advisor 2 sees high risk of the temporary solution calcifying, while Advisors 1 and 3 view the seam as a low-friction migration pathway.

Whether the bearer token is meaningful defense-in-depth or merely shifts the vulnerability from network-level trust to host-level filesystem security: Advisor 2 frames it as valuable layered defense, while Advisor 3 notes the defense is entirely contingent on local filesystem permissions.
blindspots: Token lifecycle is unaddressed: there is no defined mechanism for generation, rotation, or revocation. A compromised token requires manual file edit and service restart to invalidate.
A single shared token eliminates user-specific audit trails, making it impossible to attribute console actions to individual operators.
No discussion of how the token is securely distributed to authorized operators in a tailnet-only environment without a secure out-of-band channel.
Risk of the token file being accidentally captured by backups or served by a misconfigured static file path.
No explicit roadmap commitment or scheduled checkpoint for the OAuth migration, increasing the likelihood the temporary bearer-token posture persists indefinitely.

question: When a validated council-config deploy is accepted, when should it take effect? fusion() reads the config file fresh on every call, so (a) immediate write = the very next council consult (possibly one forced mid-build by a RUNNING stage-2 segment) silently uses the new models; (b) a two-step stage/apply split like run_stage4 (marker file, second call applies) keeps a human between validation and effect but doubles the workflow for what is usually a cheap, reversible file edit; (c) immediate write but REFUSED while the do-lock 'build' lane is held (deploys blocked mid-build, instant otherwise). Which semantics, and what should the API return in the blocked/staged case?
synthesis: Adopt Option (c): immediate write to the config file, but refuse the deploy while the do-lock 'build' lane is held. This gives instant, fusion()-visible effect in the common case with no extra workflow steps, while deterministically preventing mid-build model swaps. On success, return 200 OK with a JSON body containing the written config and the new fusion hash, so the operator gets immediate confirmation that the config is active for the next council consult. When blocked (build lock held), return 409 Conflict with a JSON body {"blocked": true, "reason": "build_locked"}, which is more semantically precise than 423 for a concurrent-state conflict and gives the UI a structured field to surface. There is no staged state and no two-step apply; the operator simply retries after the build completes.
contradictions: HTTP status for the blocked case: 423 Locked (Advisor 2) vs 409 Conflict with structured JSON (Advisor 3) vs generic text message (Advisor 1). Resolved in favor of 409 Conflict with JSON body.
Success response payload: 204 No Content (Advisor 2) vs 200 OK with written config and fusion hash (Advisor 3). Resolved in favor of 200 OK with config and hash for immediate operator confirmation.
blindspots: No force-override mechanism exists for emergency config changes during long-running builds; operators must abort the build or wait, which could block critical unrelated fixes.

The do-lock must provably cover every risky fusion() call; if a RUNNING stage-2 segment can consult the council after the lock drops but before the build fully finalizes, the immediate write races into that window.
The lock is binary—no granularity to allow deploys that only modify models unrelated to the currently running build segment, which could reduce unnecessary blocking.
